title: Chapter 20: Testimony
content: The medical board hearing was held in a gray government building downtown.
Chloe arrived at 8:00 AM. The cameras were already there.

She had expected a few reporters. What she got was a crowd. At least two dozen journalists lined the sidewalk, cameras raised, microphones ready. News vans from three major networks were parked at the curb.
"You ready for this?" Elena asked.
Chloe looked at the crowd. At the cameras. At the building where Dr. Webber would spend the day trying to save his career.
"I've been ready for thirty-two days."
She got out of the car. The cameras turned. The questions started.
She walked past them without answering and pushed through the glass doors.

The hearing room was smaller than she expected. A long table for the board members — five of them, three men and two women, all wearing expressions of practiced neutrality. A podium for witnesses. Two tables for the parties.

Dr. Webber sat at the respondent's table, flanked by two lawyers in suits that cost more than Chloe's monthly rent. He looked smaller than she remembered. The confidence was gone. In its place was the hollow alertness of a man who knew he was fighting for his professional life.
He did not look at her when she walked in.
Chloe took her seat at the petitioner's table beside Elena. Mia was in the audience, laptop open, recording everything. Tiffany and Amara sat two rows behind her.
The board chair — a woman with gray hair and reading glasses perched on her nose — called the hearing to order.

"We are here today to review the medical license of Dr. Marcus Webber in light of allegations brought by former patients of Serenity Peak Wellness Center. The board will hear testimony from witnesses and review evidence before making a determination."
She looked at Chloe.
"Ms. Hart. You are the first witness."
Chloe stood up and walked to the podium.
She placed her hand on the Bible. She did not believe in God. But she believed in the truth.
"Do you swear to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth?"
"I do."
She took her seat at the podium. The board chair adjusted her glasses.
"Please state your name for the record."
"Chloe Hart."
"Ms. Hart, you were a patient at Serenity Peak Wellness Center from May 10 to June 11 of this year. Is that correct?"
"Yes."
"Can you describe the circumstances of your admission?"
Chloe took a breath. She had rehearsed this moment a hundred times. But now that it was here, she did not need the rehearsal. The words came naturally.

"My father and my ex-boyfriend signed the admission papers without my knowledge or consent. I was told I was going to a wellness retreat. When I arrived, I was stripped of my belongings, given a hospital gown, and locked in a room with barred windows."
"Were you informed of your diagnosis?"

"I was told I had 'adjustment disorder with anxious and depressed mood.' No one ever explained what that meant. No one ever treated me for it. The only medication I was given was a sedative that made me sleep sixteen hours a day."
The board members exchanged glances.
"Did you ever see a licensed psychiatrist during your stay?"
"No. I saw Dr. Webber twice. The first time was intake. The second time was when I was being sent to the Isolation Wing for refusing medication."
"The Isolation Wing?"
"A windowless room in the basement. No furniture except a mattress on the floor. No human contact for seventy-two hours."
The room was quiet. One of the board members — a woman with sharp features and a concerned frown — wrote something in her notes.
"Thank you, Ms. Hart. The board has no further questions at this time. Counsel for Dr. Webber may cross-examine."
Webber's lawyer stood up. He was tall, silver-haired, with the relaxed confidence of a man who had done this a thousand times.
"Ms. Hart. You admit that you were brought to the facility by your own father."
"I was brought there against my will, yes."
"But your father signed the admission papers. Would you describe your father as a responsible man?"
"I would describe my father as a man who built a business on locking women up and billing their families for it."
The lawyer's smile tightened. "Objection. Non-responsive."
"Sustained," the chair said. "Ms. Hart, please answer the question directly."
"My father signed the papers. I don't believe that makes him responsible. It makes him complicit."
"Did you ever express a desire to harm yourself or others?"
"No."
"Did you ever refuse to eat?"
"No."
"Did you ever attempt to leave the facility?"
"Yes. Multiple times. Because I was being held illegally."
"And each time, the staff determined that you were a danger to yourself and returned you to your room. Is that accurate?"
"The staff determined whatever Dr. Webber told them to determine."
The lawyer was not smiling anymore.

"Ms. Hart, isn't it true that you have a history of conflict with authority figures? Your academic record shows disciplinary issues. Your employment history shows repeated terminations. Would you describe yourself as a difficult person?"
"I would describe myself as someone who doesn't obey orders just because they come from someone older."
"Would you describe yourself as someone who follows rules?"
"I follow rules that make sense. I don't follow rules designed to control me."
"So you admit you broke facility rules."
"I smuggled in a phone. I hacked the Wi-Fi. I started a patient newsletter that exposed the facility's practices. Yes. I broke their rules. Because their rules were designed to silence us."
She looked directly at Dr. Webber for the first time.
"And I'd do it again."

The cross-examination continued for another hour. But Chloe could feel the shift. The lawyer was still asking questions, but the board was no longer listening to him. They were watching her. Watching Webber. Reading the body language of two people whose power dynamic had completely inverted.
When the chair finally excused her, Chloe walked back to her seat without looking at Webber.
"You did good," Elena whispered.
"I told the truth. That's not hard."
"For some people, it is."

Chloe stayed for the rest of the hearing. She watched former staff members testify. She watched patients she had never met describe the same experiences she had lived through. She watched Dr. Webber's lawyers grow quieter and quieter as the evidence piled up.
At 4:00 PM, the board adjourned for deliberation.
The GoFundMe had raised two hundred and thirty thousand dollars.
Chloe checked the number on her phone. Two hundred and thirty-one. Two hundred and thirty-four. It was climbing in real time.
"The hearing is being livestreamed," Mia said from behind her. "Your testimony has two million views."
Chloe looked at the number again. Two hundred and forty-one thousand.
"We're going to make it," she said.
She walked out of the building at 5:00 PM.
The cameras were still there. More of them now. The crowd had doubled.
A reporter stepped forward — the same young woman from the courthouse steps. "Chloe! What happened in there?"
Chloe stopped. She looked at the cameras. At the crowd of journalists, survivors, supporters who had gathered to watch.
"Dr. Webber's license is going to be revoked," she said. "The facility is going to be sold. And I'm going to buy it."
The cameras pressed closer.
"What will you do with it?" the reporter asked.
Chloe smiled.
"Turn it into something real."
